verbatim: [§3] Prometheus: Have no dealings with her, Zeus. As sure as Neteus’s daughter conceives by you, your child shall mete you the measure you meted to— Zeus: I shall lose my kingdom, you would say?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule of succession where a son born of a specific high-status union (Zeus and the daughter of Neteus) has the power/right to overthrow the father and take the kingdom.

verbatim: [§6] Eros: Well, of course. They are but mortals; the sight of Zeus is too much for them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies mortals as a group that lacks the capacity or right to safely view the high gods, contrasting them with divine beings.

verbatim: [§12] Zeus: Well, we have no one but ourselves to blame for it: we make too much of these mortals, admitting them -to our table like this. When they drink of our nectar, and behold the beauties of Heaven (so different from those of Earth!), ’tis no wonder if they fall in love, and form ambitious schemes!
extracted_rules.reasoning: Zeus describes the admission of mortals to the gods' table as an unusual exception to a general boundary, implying that mortals typically lack the right to consume nectar or inhabit heaven.

verbatim: [§12] Hera: He [Eros] has made himself master of you; no doubt of that. He does what he likes with you;—leads you by the nose. You follow him whither he chooses, and assume every shape at his command; you are his chattel, his toy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Hera uses the legal language of slavery ('chattel', 'at his command') to describe the loss of autonomy gods experience under the influence of the personified criterion of Love.

verbatim: [§14] Zeus If he says anything of the kind he shall soon find himself in Hades, spinning round on a wheel for all eternity. That will keep him busy! And serve him right; not for falling in love—I see no great harm in that—but for letting his tongue wag.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific rule for Ixion (a king) where the resource of protection from eternal torture is lost specifically due to the act of boasting about a goddess, representing a normative constraint on mortals interacting with divinity.

verbatim: [§15] Hephaestus Why, what harm can it do, only just born? Apollo Ask Posidon; it stole his trident. Ask Ares; he was surprised to find his sword gone out of the scabbard. ... Hephaestus Never! that infant? he has hardly found his legs yet; he is not out of his baby-linen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Hephaestus argues that an infant (Maia's baby) cannot be held responsible for harm or mischief due to its developmental state, reflecting a societal rule where age grants exemption from the expectations of adult behavior or labor.

verbatim: [§20] Zeus Impossible! She is determined to remain a maid for ever. Not that I have any objection, personally. Hephaestus That is all I want. You can leave the rest tome. I'll carry her off this moment. Zeus Well, if you think it so easy. But I am sure it is a hopeless case.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the dialogue regarding Athena, Zeus acknowledges a woman's determination to remain a 'maid' (virgin) as a valid state that he, as the patriarchal head, does not object to, establishing a right to refuse marriage.

verbatim: [§22] Aphrodite ...and before now I have smacked his little behind with my slipper. It is no use; he is frightened and cries for a minute or two, and then forgets all about it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aphrodite describes a routine practice of using physical discipline (smacking with a slipper) on her young son Eros, indicating that minors in this societal structure lack protection from maternal corporal punishment.

verbatim: [§25] Aphrodite But Rhea! how could you dare to set her on thinking of that young fellow in Phrygia, an old lady like her, the mother of so many Gods? Why, you have made her quite mad
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aphrodite expresses a social norm/opinion that it is inappropriate or 'mad' for an 'old lady' to pursue a young man, suggesting a loss of the resource of romantic agency based on the criterion of age.

verbatim: [§28] Zeus: Cease, I say, and let us hear ourselves speak, or I will send you both away from table. Heracles, Asclepius died before you, and has the right to a better place.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Zeus establishes a specific rule for seating at the table of the Gods based on the chronology of death (seniority in the afterlife), granting Asclepius a superior position over Heracles because he died first.

verbatim: [§38] Leto: You set no small value on yourself, madam, because you are the wife of Zeus, and share his throne; you may insult whom you please.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies that Hera's status as the wife of the king (Zeus) and sharer of the throne grants her the perceived social right or power to insult those of lower standing without immediate consequence.

verbatim: [§38] Hera: those oracle-shops that he has opened at Delphi, and Clarus, and Dindyma, are a cheat; he takes good care to be on the safe side by giving ambiguous answers that no one can understand, and makes money out of it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Developing from the occupation of prophecy/divination, the text describes the established practice of operating 'oracle-shops' to generate revenue from the public.

verbatim: [§41] Well, Zeus, I should be ashamed if I had such a son; so effeminate, and so given to drinking; tying up his hair in a ribbon, indeed! and spending most of his time among mad women, himself as much a woman as any of them; dancing to flute and drum and cymbal! He resembles any one rather than his father.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Hera's critique of Dionysus establishes a social norm where men are expected to avoid 'effeminate' behaviors like wearing ribbons or participating in female-dominated activities, suggesting a loss of social standing or parental 'pride' if these boundaries are crossed.

verbatim: [§44] Eros: I am afraid of her, mother; those awful flashing eyes! she is like a man, only worse. When I go against her with my arrow on the string, a toss of her plume frightens me; my hand shakes so that it drops the bow.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text highlights Athena's 'man-like' qualities as an aberration that inspires fear and stops the natural flow of Eros's influence. It suggests that women who adopt martial or masculine appearances are viewed as outside the norm of their gender.

verbatim: [§46] Aphrodite: But why do you let the Muses go scot free? do they toss their plumes and hold out Gorgons’ heads? Eros: Ah, mother, they make me bashful; they are so grand, always studying and composing; I love to stand there listening to their music.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Muses (representing the life of study and composition) are described as being left 'scot free' by Eros. This indicates that their dedication to education and the arts provides a social or symbolic protection from the typical distractions of desire/romance.

verbatim: [§47/§48] But this young Phrygian to whom you are going is of the royal blood —a relation of Ganymede’s,—and at the same time a simple countryman; so that we need have no hesitation in trusting his eyes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Zeus argues that Paris is a trustworthy judge of beauty specifically because of his lineage (royal blood), implying that high birth grants an inherent capacity for discernment or or higher status in judicial matters.

verbatim: [§47] But I have nothing to do with fighting, Athene. As you see, there is peace throughout all Lydia and Phrygia... I am not going to take your present... You can robe again and put on your helmet; I have seen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paris rejects Athene's offer of military glory, and Athene is depicted as 'intimidating' or out of place in her martial attire during a beauty contest, reflecting a societal norm where combat and military ambition are characterized as masculine or separate from the female sphere.

verbatim: [§47] But that is what I cannot believe,—that she will forsake her husband to cross the seas with a stranger, a barbarian.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paris himself expresses the rule/norm that a Greek woman of high status (Helen) would not naturally leave her home for a 'barbarian', acknowledging a barrier based on ethnic/cultural identity.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§51] But he is a young man, so it is to be presumed that distinction on the field of battle is among his desires.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a societal norm where youth is inherently linked to the pursuit of military glory and the expectation of serving on the battlefield.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§54] This is no sight for a herdsman’s eyes; let the fine city folk decide on such matters. As for me, I can tell you which of two goats is the fairer beast; or I can judge betwixt heifer and heifer;—’tis my trade.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Paris identifies his occupation (herdsman/rustic) as a boundary that excludes him from high-level judgments (beauty/law) which are reserved for the 'fine city folk'.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§67] And why should the sons of Alcmena and Semele, paltry women, why should they feast at their ease, and I—the son of Maia, the grandson of Atlas—wait upon them?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Hermes complains that despite his superior lineage (grandson of Atlas), he is forced into manual service ('wait upon them') while those from 'paltry' lineage (sons of mortal women) feast. This reflects a rule regarding the assignment of labor based on status.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§67] Mortal slaves are better off than I am: they have the chance of being sold to a new master; I wish I had the same!
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions a right or 'chance' that mortal slaves possess—the ability to be sold to a different master—which Hermes (in his divine servitude) lacks.

extracted_rules.rule: Filial obedience to avoid corporal punishment
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§67] Come, come, child. You must do as your father bids you, like a good boy. Run along now to Argos and Boeotia; don’t loiter, or you will get a whipping.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that children (even divine ones) are subject to corporal punishment ('a whipping') if they fail to obey parental commands and perform assigned tasks.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be sold to new masters
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§69] Mortal slaves are better off than I am: they have the chance of being sold to a new master; I wish I had the same!
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions that mortal slaves have the opportunity to change masters through sale, a right or possibility that Hermes envies, indicating a standard practice in the contemporary society described.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§69] You must do as your father bids you, like a good boy. Run along now to Argos and Boeotia; don’t loiter, or you will get a whipping.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Maia warns Hermes that failing to obey his father's orders will result in a whipping, reflecting the societal norm where children/sons are subject to physical discipline by parents.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§69] And why should the sons of Alcmena and Semele, paltry women, why should they feast at their ease, and I—the son of Maia, the grandson of Atlas—wait upon them?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Hermes complains about the unequal distribution of labor based on the status of one's mother (lineage), where some sons are permitted to feast while others of supposedly higher lineage are forced to serve them.
